results.possibleIdentity: Helga Meyer was a sculptor and administrator active in the New York City art scene during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is historically recognized for her figurative bronze sculptures, including a solo exhibition titled "Sculpture by Helga Meyer" held at the Nicholas Roerich Museum in New York in 1969. One of her notable works from this period is "Head of a Man" (1969), a bronze sculpture that is sometimes described as a portrait of Picasso. In February 1970, her sculpture "Freedom Fighter" was featured on the cover of The Crisis, the official journal of the NAACP. Meyer was also an integral staff member of the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), where she worked in the New York office as a coordinator for the Technical Services Program. In this capacity, she assisted in the organization's core mission of matching artists with engineers and scientists to facilitate interdisciplinary collaborations. Her name appears in various historical checklists and indices of African American artists active during the Civil Rights and Black Arts Movements of the 1960s and 70s.

results.possibleIdentity: Joseph "Sweet Joe" Russell (1939–2012) was an American tenor vocalist and a founding member of the influential a cappella group The Persuasions. Formed in Brooklyn in 1962, the group became prominent in the late 1960s and early 1970s after being discovered by Frank Zappa, who signed them to his Bizarre/Straight label in 1969. The Persuasions were known for their gospel-inflected harmonies and were often called the "kings of a cappella." Their debut album, Acappella (1970), and subsequent works like Street Corner Symphony (1971) and We Came to Play (1971) established them as innovators in the vocal arts. The group performed at major venues such as Carnegie Hall in 1970 and were noted for their ability to interpret diverse musical genres, including rock, soul, and pop, without instrumental accompaniment. While the group's direct association with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization is not explicitly detailed in available historical credits, they were deeply embedded in the experimental and avant-garde music scene of the era, notably through their collaboration with Frank Zappa, who was himself connected to technologically innovative music production.

Joe Russell is a producer and actor who has been active in the performing arts since the late 1970s, with a career that transitioned from engineering to acting. He worked as an engineer for Southwestern Bell (S.W. Bell) from 1978 to 1980, a period following the peak of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which was founded by engineers from the Bell System (Bell Labs). Russell eventually pursued acting and producing, contributing to theater, film, and commercials. His production credits include the Netflix film XOXO (2016) and the short film Laboratory Conditions. He received training in New York under Anne Jackson and Eli Wallach and has performed in various theater productions ranging from the late 1970s through the 1980s. This identity aligns with a professional background in both engineering and the arts, similar to the collaborative focus of E.A.T.

Joe G. Russell (1926–2008) was a Texas-based artist known for his oil paintings of hill country landscapes, historical portraiture, and western scenes. While he began his artistic career earlier, he dedicated himself to painting full-time starting in 1966 when he moved to Kerrville, Texas. Throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he was active in the gallery scenes of Houston and Austin and became a member of the Texas Cowboy Artists Association in 1981. Although his work primarily follows a traditional representational style rather than the technologically experimental nature associated with E.A.T., his period of peak activity coincides with the timeframe specified.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Chapman is an American designer and artist active in the late 1960s. In 1969, he co-designed the abstract board game 'Connect' with the renowned British graphic designer Ken Garland for the James Galt Toy Company. The game, which utilized a template-based grid system to create interlocking patterns, has been featured in design exhibitions and retrospectives. Given the time period and the modular, system-based nature of the project, this work aligns with the interdisciplinary goals of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization.

Robert Chapman was an artist and professional assistant active in the New York art world during the late 20th century. He is best known for his long-term association with the Abstract Expressionist painter Willem de Kooning. Chapman served as a studio assistant, helping to prepare canvases and manage the technical aspects of de Kooning's work. In historical accounts, he has provided detailed recollections of de Kooning's creative processes and mental health challenges during the 1970s and 1980s.

Robert Chapman was an experimental researcher and psychologist active in the 1950s and 1960s who specialized in man-machine systems and simulations. Working at institutions like the RAND Corporation, he co-developed models for studying human behavior under stress within complex technological systems. His focus on the integration of human operators with hardware components and his interdisciplinary 'organism/system' approach reflects the scientific and engineering side of the collaborations fostered by Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: Alice Straus (also identified as Alice Cornwell Straus) was an administrator and coordinator associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) during the late 1960s and early 1970s. She is historically significant for her role in managing the E.A.T. Matching Service, a foundational program of the organization that used a computer database to pair artists with engineers and scientists for collaborative projects. Straus was active in the New York office of E.A.T., working alongside founders Billy Klüver and Robert Rauschenberg. She is credited in historical documentation, such as the 1972 book 'Pavilion' (edited by Klüver, Martin, and Rose), for her contributions to the matching system and the coordination of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan. In the 1970s, she was also associated with the Gambier Experimental College (G.E.C.) at Kenyon College, where she served as an instructor and later held various administrative roles, including assistant director of development and alumni affairs. Her background includes work as a computer-systems analyst, a skillset that likely supported her technical coordination work within the E.A.T. matching system.

results.possibleIdentity: Terry Randall Anderson is an American musician and songwriter best known as the drummer for the electric blues and psychedelic rock band A.B. Skhy. Formed in Milwaukee in 1968, the band relocated to San Francisco and was active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. They released a self-titled debut album in 1969 and a follow-up, 'Ramblin' On', in 1970. The band was part of the San Francisco psychedelic music scene, performing at venues like the Avalon Ballroom and collaborating with musicians such as Howard Wales. Anderson is also recognized as the songwriter of 'Battleship Chains', made famous by The Georgia Satellites.

Terry H. Anderson is a prominent historian and professor at Texas A&M University who specializes in the history of the 1960s and 1970s. He is the author of 'The Movement and the Sixties' (1995), a definitive history of the decade's social activism, counterculture, and reform movements. While not a visual artist himself, his work is frequently cited in art historical contexts that discuss the 'Experiments in Art and Technology' (E.A.T.) and other radical art-worker movements of the era, as he chronicles the cultural shifts that allowed such collaborations between artists and engineers to flourish.

Terry Anderson was a radio figure and DJ active in the 1970s, associated with London-based pirate radio stations such as Skyport and Radio Jackie. He is noted in sound and radio art research (notably by artist Magz Hall) for his participation in these experimental and 'left-wing' broadcasting platforms, which explored the artistic and social potential of radio technology outside of traditional settings during the 1970s.

results.possibleIdentity: David Puchkoff is a New York-based real estate developer, builder, and technical designer who has been active in the city's creative and technical communities since at least the late 1960s. He is best known for designing and constructing a unique 'rooftop cottage' and a 1,200-square-foot sedum meadow on top of his six-story loft building at 131 Spring Street in the SoHo/Greenwich Village area. This project, which includes a rustic cabin and a country-style porch, has been widely cited for its innovative architectural and artistic integration of nature into the urban environment. Puchkoff and his wife, writer Eileen Stukane, have frequently hosted artistic and musical events at this location, including notable tributes to Joni Mitchell. Historically, Puchkoff has been an active member and advocate for the Herreshoff 'S' Boat racing class, with records showing his involvement in preserving the fleet's legacy during the late 1960s and early 1970s. His background as a developer and builder in New York during the era of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)—an organization that flourished in SoHo and prioritized collaborations between artists and technical experts—positions him as a likely collaborator or technical artist within that movement.

results.possibleIdentity: Julie Katz was an artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, primarily known for her collaborations in the early video art and electronic media movement in New York. She is specifically credited alongside artist Tom DeWitt for designing and providing the cover photography for the second issue of Radical Software (1970), a seminal journal dedicated to video as an artistic and social medium. Radical Software was published by the Raindance Corporation, a collective of which she and DeWitt were associates. This circle was closely linked to the broader art and technology movement of the era, which included Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Her work is featured in historical surveys of the period, such as the Tate's 'Electric Dreams: Art and Technology Before the Internet' (2024), which maps the connections between artists, engineers, and collectives like Raindance and E.A.T. active between 1967 and 1972.

wikidata_candidates.reasoning: The names are an exact match. The Wikidata entity is a choreographer, which fits the artist context, and her career (including the formation of the Multigravitational Aerodance Group) was active during the 1970s, aligning perfectly with the date on the card.

results.possibleIdentity: Stephanie Evanitsky is a pioneering American choreographer and artist recognized as a founder of aerial dance. Active primarily in the late 1960s and 1970s, she established the Multigravitational Experiment Group (later the Multigravitational Aerodance Group) in New York City around 1969. Her work was characterized by the use of suspended apparatuses—such as rope webs, scaffolding, tire tubes, and harnesses—to explore movement in three-dimensional space and the illusion of weightlessness, often referred to as 'Aerodance.' Influenced by the multimedia innovations of Alwin Nikolais, Evanitsky's approach bridged the gap between dance and sculpture, making her work highly receptive within the visual arts community. She received a Guggenheim Fellowship in 1976 (some sources cite 1974) for her choreography. Notable performances included a series at the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) in 1971. Although her specific affiliation with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is suggested by the experimental nature and technical requirements of her rigging, her primary legacy is the creation of the first East Coast aerial dance company, which continued under different leadership until the mid-1980s.

results.possibleIdentity: David Lawless was a New York-based artist active in the mid-to-late 1960s and early 1970s. He was a student at the Pratt Institute in the mid-1960s and was part of a pivotal group of students and staff—including Richard Castellana, Joseph Eletz, Larry Faden, Marjorie Kramer, and Irene Peslikis—who broke away from the traditional art school curriculum at Pratt and other institutions to co-found the New York Studio School of Drawing, Painting and Sculpture in 1964. During the late 1960s, Lawless was involved in the New York art activism scene and is documented as a participant in the Art Workers' Coalition (AWC) and the Ad Hoc Women's Artists' Committee. His name appears alongside prominent figures such as Roy Lichtenstein, Mercedes Matter, and Robert Motherwell in lists of artists who engaged in political and anti-war advocacy, such as the protest efforts against the Vietnam War. Given his presence in the New York avant-garde and experimental circles of the time, he is potentially associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which frequently collaborated with artists from these same social and professional networks to bridge the gap between artistic practice and technological engineering.
